chunk_summary: Guidelines for trace element and vitamin dosing in pediatric parenteral nutrition, with specific modifications for cholestasis, including adjusted manganese and copper management and increased monitoring for high-output losses.
learning_objectives: Understand routine trace element dosing and condition-specific modifications in pediatric parenteral nutrition.
Learn cholestasis-specific adjustments for manganese and copper dosing in parenteral nutrition.
Recognize the importance of monitoring serum zinc and alkaline phosphatase in high-output losses.
Prescribe daily IV multivitamins containing water- and fat-soluble vitamins for pediatric patients on long-term PN.
key_facts: Zinc dosing: 250 micrograms/kg/day for term infants up to 3 months, 100 micrograms/kg/day for 3–12 months, and 50 micrograms/kg/day for children older than 12 months (maximum 5 mg/day).
Copper dosing: 20 micrograms/kg/day for term infants and children (maximum 0.5 mg/day).
Selenium dosing: 2–3 micrograms/kg/day for term infants and children (maximum 100 micrograms/day).
Manganese dosing: No more than 1 microgram/kg/day (maximum 50 micrograms/day) due to tissue deposition risk; discontinue in cholestasis.
Cholestasis management: Avoid reflex zero-copper dosing; monitor copper status and individualize dosing.
reference_quote: Prescribe a daily IV multivitamin containing water- and fat-soluble vitamins from the outset whenever possible.
document_key_topics: Trace element dosing in pediatric parenteral nutrition
Cholestasis-specific modifications for manganese and copper
Vitamin provision in long-term parenteral nutrition
Monitoring and adjustments for high-output losses
